prompt: Please provide a detailed explanation of the differences and connections between Google's recently released A2A protocol and the MCP protocol. Furthermore, elaborate on the innovative aspects of the A2A protocol and the specific problems it is designed to address.
article:
## 1) What each protocol is *for* (scope + mental model)

### A2A (Agent2Agent): **agent ↔ agent interoperability**
A2A is an open protocol intended to let *independent* AI agent systems (often from different vendors, built with different frameworks, running on different servers) **discover each other, negotiate how to interact, and collaborate on tasks**—without needing access to each other’s internal memory, tools, or proprietary “thought process” (“opaque execution”) [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[2]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A). Google’s launch framing is explicitly enterprise/multi-agent: organizations will deploy many agents across siloed apps/data; productivity gains depend on those agents working together across vendors and platforms [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/).

### MCP (Model Context Protocol): **LLM app/agent ↔ tools & data**
MCP is an open protocol to standardize how an LLM application (an “MCP host”) connects to *external tools and context providers* (“MCP servers”), exposing **tools, resources, and prompts** via JSON-RPC so the host can safely and consistently integrate them [[26]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25), [[29]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/architecture). MCP’s mental model is “N×M integration becomes N+M”: many apps connect to many tool/data providers through one standard interface [[27]](https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol).

**Key takeaway:** A2A standardizes *collaboration between agents.* MCP standardizes *how one agent/app uses tools and retrieves context.* They overlap in infrastructure choices (JSON-RPC, HTTP, SSE) but differ in abstraction level and what is being connected.

---

## 2) Core architectural differences (high level)

### 2.1 Roles and trust boundaries

| Dimension | A2A | MCP |
|---|---|---|
| Primary interaction | **Client agent** asks a **remote agent** to do work, track progress, return artifacts | **Host** runs one or more **clients** that connect 1:1 to **servers** providing tools/resources/prompts |
| Assumption about “the other side” | Remote agent may be **opaque** and independently operated; A2A is designed for *inter-agent* collaboration across org boundaries [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[2]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A) | MCP servers are *capability providers*; host mediates what context/tools are exposed to the model and enforces isolation between servers [[29]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/architecture) |
| Where conversation history lives | A2A can return task history via protocol fields; tasks/contexts are first-class [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/) | Full conversation history stays with the **host**; servers should only receive necessary context [[29]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/architecture) |

### 2.2 What is standardized: “task collaboration” vs “tool primitives”

- **A2A standardizes**: discovery (Agent Cards), task lifecycle, messages and multi-part content, artifacts as outputs, streaming/push notifications for long-running work, and multi-binding equivalence (JSON-RPC, gRPC, HTTP/REST) [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).  
- **MCP standardizes**: initialization + capability negotiation, and primitives/tools:  
  - **Tools** (callable actions with JSON Schema I/O) [[32]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/tools)  
  - **Resources** (URI-addressable content) [[33]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/resources)  
  - **Prompts** (templated message sets) [[34]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/prompts)  
  plus client-side capabilities like sampling, roots, elicitation, and transport details (stdio, Streamable HTTP) [[30]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/lifecycle), [[37]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/transports).

### 2.3 Discovery

- **A2A discovery** is a first-class protocol feature: an agent publishes an **Agent Card** (well-known JSON metadata) describing identity, skills, interfaces/transports, and auth requirements [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/).  
- **MCP discovery** is typically configured by the host (e.g., “install this MCP server”), and *public discovery* is mediated by an **MCP Registry** (metadata catalog + publishing governance), rather than a mandatory well-known endpoint on every server [[47]](https://modelcontextprotocol.info/tools/registry/faq/), [[110]](https://blog.modelcontextprotocol.io/posts/2025-09-08-mcp-registry-preview/).

---

## 3) A2A in detail (what it standardizes)

### 3.1 Canonical model + multi-binding design (an interoperability play)
A2A’s spec is explicitly layered:
1) **Canonical data model** (protobuf-defined objects),  
2) **Abstract operations**,  
3) **Protocol bindings** (JSON-RPC, gRPC, HTTP+JSON/REST), with the proto as the normative source [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[91]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/blob/main/docs/specification.md).  

This is a major difference from many “single transport” agent protocols: A2A is built so enterprises can choose transports (JSON-RPC over HTTP, gRPC, REST) while preserving core semantics across them [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/). Tooling reinforces this with a **Technology Compatibility Kit (TCK)** that tests compliance and transport equivalence (JSON-RPC/gRPC/REST) [[20]](https://github.com/a2aproject/a2a-tck).

### 3.2 Task-first collaboration model (async by design)

A2A’s fundamental unit of work is a **Task**, which has:
- an id and context_id,
- a **status** (state machine),
- **artifacts** (outputs),
- optional **history** (messages) [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).

**Task states** (v1.0 RC proto) include: submitted, working, completed, failed, canceled, input_required, rejected, auth_required [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/). This directly targets enterprise realities: long-running workflows, approvals, missing inputs, or delegated authentication steps. Google’s launch post calls this out as supporting tasks that can take “hours or even days,” with real-time feedback and state updates [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/).

A2A’s core RPC surface (v1.0 RC) reflects that model: SendMessage, SendStreamingMessage, GetTask/ListTasks, CancelTask, SubscribeToTask, plus push-notification config operations and GetExtendedAgentCard [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).

Notable configuration knobs in SendMessage requests include:
- `blocking` (wait until terminal or interrupted state),
- `history_length` (limit how much history to return),
- `accepted_output_modes`,
- optional `push_notification_config` [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).

### 3.3 Rich message content via “Parts” + outputs via “Artifacts”
A2A messages contain an array of **Parts**, each of which can be:
- text,
- raw bytes (base64 in JSON),
- URL references,
- structured JSON values [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).

This enables “modality agnostic” exchange (text + files + structured data), and Google positions it as supporting UX negotiation (e.g., richer UI components, forms, media) [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/). Task outputs are returned as **Artifacts**, each containing Parts [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).

### 3.4 Streaming and push notifications: designed for long-running work
A2A explicitly supports:
- synchronous request/response,
- **streaming** updates (commonly via SSE for JSON-RPC/REST binding),
- **push notifications** for long-running or disconnected clients [[2]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A), [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/), [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).

A notable evolution: **v0.3.0** task status update events included a `final` boolean [[95]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/raw/refs/tags/v0.3.0/specification/grpc/a2a.proto), but **v1.0 RC** removes that (field reserved) and relies on terminal task states + stream closure to signal completion [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/). This is important for implementers because it changes how clients detect end-of-stream.

Push notifications are configured per task via a TaskPushNotificationConfig resource set; config includes URL, token, and authentication scheme/credentials [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).

### 3.5 Agent discovery via Agent Cards (and why it matters)

A2A Agent Cards are central: they advertise identity, skills/capabilities, transport endpoints, and auth requirements [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/). The protocol supports:
- a public Agent Card at a well-known URI (path changed in v0.3.0 from `/.well-known/agent.json` to `/.well-known/agent-card.json`) [[4]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/releases), [[5]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/roadmap/),
- **extended agent cards** retrievable with authentication for more sensitive metadata [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[4]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/releases).

A2A also added **Agent Card signatures** (v0.3.0) to enable integrity verification of the discovery document [[4]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/releases). The v0.3.0 spec defines JWS-based signature structures for cards [[94]](https://a2a-protocol.org/v0.3.0/specification/).

### 3.6 Extensions: growth without forking the core
A2A has a first-class **extensions** mechanism where:
- extensions are identified by URIs,
- agents declare support in their cards,
- clients can activate them via an `A2A-Extensions` header and the server replies with activated extensions [[11]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/topics/extensions/).  
Extensions can be data-only, add methods, overlay profiles, or even add state-machine requirements—without changing core data structures or enums [[11]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/topics/extensions/). Google highlights extensions enabling capabilities like traceability (end-to-end workflow visibility across nested agent invocations) and domain/ops features (e.g., latency-aware routing hints) [[15]](https://developers.googleblog.com/a2a-extensions-empowering-custom-agent-functionality/).

---

## 4) MCP in detail (what it standardizes)

### 4.1 Host/client/server architecture + lifecycle handshake
MCP defines:
- **Hosts**: the LLM application container that manages permissions, aggregates context, and coordinates multiple clients,
- **Clients**: per-server connectors (1:1 relationship per server),
- **Servers**: tool/context providers [[26]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25), [[29]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/architecture).

A rigorous lifecycle starts with `initialize` (version + capabilities + implementation info), then `notifications/initialized` [[30]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/lifecycle). Version negotiation rules are explicit; for HTTP transports, clients must send `MCP-Protocol-Version` after negotiation [[30]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/lifecycle), [[37]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/transports).

### 4.2 Server primitives: tools, resources, prompts (typed + discoverable)
MCP servers expose three main “primitives”:

**Tools**: discoverable via `tools/list`, callable via `tools/call`, defined with JSON Schema inputSchema (and optionally outputSchema), returning structured/unstructured content blocks [[32]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/tools). MCP emphasizes that hosts should provide human-in-the-loop controls for tool calls (UX not mandated, but strongly recommended) [[32]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/tools).

**Resources**: URI-addressed context, discovered via `resources/list`, fetched via `resources/read`, with optional `resources/subscribe` for updates and templates via `resources/templates/list` [[33]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/resources).

**Prompts**: templates retrieved via `prompts/list` and `prompts/get`, intended for user-controlled invocation [[34]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/prompts).

### 4.3 Client capabilities: sampling, roots, elicitation
MCP also standardizes capabilities where **servers can ask the host/client** to do things:
- **Sampling**: server requests the host to run an LLM completion while the host retains control (permissions, review) [[35]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/learn/client-concepts).
- **Roots**: host declares filesystem-like boundaries (coordination mechanism, not a security boundary) [[35]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/learn/client-concepts), [[36]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/client/roots).
- **Elicitation**: server requests structured user input, validated against schema [[35]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/learn/client-concepts).

### 4.4 Transports: stdio + Streamable HTTP (with explicit SSE/resume semantics)
MCP defines two standard transports:
- **stdio** (local subprocess; newline-delimited JSON-RPC) [[37]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/transports)
- **Streamable HTTP**, which can return either a single JSON response or an SSE stream; includes reconnection, retry, session identifiers (`MCP-Session-Id`), and a strong security warning to validate Origin headers to prevent DNS rebinding [[37]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/transports).  

This is one area where MCP is “lower-level” than A2A: the transport spec is very explicit about SSE resumption, polling behavior, Last-Event-ID replay constraints, and session termination via HTTP DELETE [[37]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/transports).

### 4.5 Authorization: OAuth-centric framework for HTTP transports
MCP provides an optional-but-standard OAuth-based authorization spec for HTTP transports [[44]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/authorization). It requires mechanisms like protected resource metadata discovery (RFC 9728), authorization server metadata discovery (RFC 8414 and/or OIDC discovery), and recommended client registration options like Client ID Metadata Documents [[44]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/authorization). The goal is interoperability when hosts connect to servers without pre-established trust.

### 4.6 Registry: a governance/metadata layer for discovery and trust signals
MCP has an official registry that catalogs server metadata (not code), supports public and private subregistries, and includes community moderation/denylisting [[110]](https://blog.modelcontextprotocol.io/posts/2025-09-08-mcp-registry-preview/). The official registry applies strict requirements around namespace ownership, package ownership verification, and limits which publisher-provided `_meta` is preserved [[109]](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/refs/heads/main/docs/reference/server-json/official-registry-requirements.md). Publishing authentication supports GitHub-based namespaces and domain-based namespaces using Ed25519 keys proven via DNS TXT records or HTTP well-known endpoints [[108]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/registry/authentication). This registry layer is a major ecosystem differentiator: MCP’s “discoverability + some provenance controls” is heavily registry-mediated.

### 4.7 MCP “tasks” (experimental) — how it overlaps with A2A’s async goals
MCP 2025-11-25 introduces **experimental tasks** for durable operations with polling and deferred result retrieval [[42]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog), [[101]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/utilities/tasks). Task capabilities are negotiated in initialization and can wrap requests like tools/call so the server can respond quickly with a task handle, later providing results via `tasks/result` and status updates via `notifications/tasks/status` [[101]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/utilities/tasks), [[100]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/schema). Conceptually, this is MCP moving toward some of the async lifecycle semantics A2A was designed around from day one—but in MCP it is explicitly framed as experimental and capability-negotiated [[42]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog), [[101]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/utilities/tasks).

---

## 5) How A2A and MCP connect (complementarity + layering patterns)

### 5.1 The “stacking” model: A2A **between agents**, MCP **inside agents**
Google’s A2A launch explicitly says A2A is **complementary** to MCP: MCP gives agents tools and context; A2A connects agents to other agents in large-scale multi-agent deployments [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/). The A2A repo itself points to learning how A2A complements MCP [[2]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A). Many ecosystem explainers converge on the same architecture: each agent may use MCP servers for its internal tool/data access, while A2A lets agents orchestrate and delegate to other agents across org and vendor boundaries [[63]](https://auth0.com/blog/mcp-vs-a2a/).

A practical way to think about it:

- **MCP answers:** “How does this agent/app call tools and fetch data safely and portably?”  
- **A2A answers:** “How do multiple agents coordinate responsibilities, progress, and handoffs across systems and vendors?”

### 5.2 Bridging patterns in practice

**Pattern A: “Remote agent uses MCP internally” (most common hybrid)**
1) A2A client agent discovers remote agent via Agent Card.  
2) Client sends task via A2A.  
3) Remote agent, to complete the task, uses MCP to call its tools/resources, then returns artifacts/status via A2A.  

This pattern is demonstrated in community integrations that explicitly implement the A2A server while the agent’s capability execution is via MCP tool calls [[71]](https://a2aprotocol.ai/docs/guide/a2a-mcp-integration), [[70]](https://github.com/sing1ee/a2a-mcp-openrouter).

**Pattern B: “Wrap MCP servers behind an A2A façade” (A2A as a universal interface)**
Some demos propose treating “tools as agents” by putting an A2A wrapper in front of MCP tool servers, so the outer orchestration layer only speaks A2A while individual capabilities are fulfilled by MCP tool invocations behind the scenes [[71]](https://a2aprotocol.ai/docs/guide/a2a-mcp-integration), [[70]](https://github.com/sing1ee/a2a-mcp-openrouter). This can simplify multi-agent orchestration logic when you want *one* interop protocol at the top.

**Pattern C: Unified catalogs/portals listing A2A agents, MCP servers, and REST APIs**
Google’s “Agentic Portal Quickstart” describes a portal/catalog intended to unify discovery of MCP servers, A2A interfaces, and standard REST APIs, backed by Apigee API Hub and an AI gateway for governance and credentialing [[90]](https://discuss.google.dev/t/agentic-portal-quickstart-for-mcp-a2a-rest-apis/288750). This is not an on-the-wire bridge, but it’s an ecosystem “control plane” integration that reflects how enterprises actually operationalize these protocols.

---

## 6) The genuinely innovative aspects of A2A (relative to MCP and to pre-standard agent integrations)

### 6.1 A2A treats “opaque agents” as first-class interoperable components
A2A’s core premise is interoperability **without requiring shared internal state, memory, or tool exposure** [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[2]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A). That’s a different stance from many integrations where “another agent” is just invoked as a tool/function. A2A formalizes agent collaboration as peer interaction with discoverable skills, tasks, progress, and artifacts [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/).

**Why this is innovative:** it’s explicitly designed for cross-vendor ecosystems where you cannot assume transparency or shared frameworks.

### 6.2 Task lifecycle as the organizing primitive (not just request/response)
A2A is “async-first” and task-oriented, including long-running execution, interruptions (input_required/auth_required), and multiple delivery modes (blocking, streaming, push notifications) [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/), [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/). MCP historically centered on tool calls/resources within a session; only later did MCP add experimental tasks [[42]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog), [[101]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/utilities/tasks).

**Innovation:** A2A’s data model and method set are built around real enterprise workflow patterns—approvals, missing inputs, delegated auth, and long-lived coordination—rather than assuming tools return quickly.

### 6.3 Multi-modal/UX negotiation through message Parts
Google’s A2A announcement highlights “parts” as a mechanism for agents to negotiate output modalities and UX capabilities (forms, media, etc.) [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/). In the v1.0 RC proto, Parts can be text, bytes, URLs, or structured JSON values [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).

**Innovation:** A2A standardizes *content composition and delivery* for agent-to-agent outputs (including structured payloads and file/media handoffs) as a core protocol element, not an ad-hoc convention.

### 6.4 Discovery designed for runtime selection and routing (Agent Cards + interfaces)
A2A’s Agent Card mechanism supports runtime capability discovery and interface selection across multiple bindings/transports [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/). This is designed for agent routing (“find the best agent for this task”) at runtime [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/), and is supported by protocol constructs like multiple interface declarations and version selection semantics (especially as the spec matures) [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[7]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/whats-new-v1/).

### 6.5 Security posture: enterprise-ready discovery integrity + auth scheme declaration
A2A has evolved toward enterprise-grade discovery integrity:
- Agent Card signing support added (v0.3.0) [[4]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/releases), with JWS structures in the spec [[94]](https://a2a-protocol.org/v0.3.0/specification/).
- Security schemes (including mTLS support added in v0.3.0 changes) evolved as the protocol stabilized [[4]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/releases).  
Google’s launch emphasizes “secure by default,” with auth scheme parity to OpenAPI-like schemes [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/).

**Innovation:** treating “how to authenticate to this agent” as part of the interoperable discovery contract, and adding integrity controls for the discovery document itself.

### 6.6 Extensibility that is explicit and negotiable at runtime
A2A’s extensions are URI-identified, declared in Agent Cards, and activated via headers, with support for “required” extensions that can cause request rejection if the client doesn’t comply [[11]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/topics/extensions/). Google highlights extensions enabling traceability and specialized operational behaviors [[15]](https://developers.googleblog.com/a2a-extensions-empowering-custom-agent-functionality/).

**Innovation:** A2A attempts to prevent “ecosystem fragmentation” by making extensions a designed, negotiated surface rather than a proliferation of incompatible proprietary fields.

### 6.7 Multi-binding equivalence and conformance tooling (TCK/Inspector)
A2A’s commitment to multiple bindings (JSON-RPC/gRPC/REST) plus conformance tooling (Inspector, TCK) indicates a “real standard” posture aimed at interoperability across implementations and transports [[19]](https://github.com/a2aproject/a2a-inspector), [[20]](https://github.com/a2aproject/a2a-tck). That’s particularly relevant in enterprise environments where gRPC and REST are common alongside JSON-RPC.

---

## 7) What problems A2A is designed to address (and how its mechanisms map to them)

### 7.1 Problem: Multi-agent silos and vendor lock-in
**Problem statement:** enterprises will have many agents across platforms; if each is proprietary, collaboration becomes bespoke and brittle [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/).  
**A2A mechanism:** a common discovery + interaction protocol (Agent Cards, shared operations, canonical task/message model) lets agents from different vendors collaborate without custom adapters [[2]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A), [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/), [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/). Linux Foundation donation and multi-vendor backing are explicitly framed as reducing lock-in and ensuring neutral governance [[16]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/google-cloud-donates-a2a-to-linux-foundation/), [[17]](https://www.linuxfoundation.org/press/linux-foundation-launches-the-agent2agent-protocol-project-to-enable-secure-intelligent-communication-between-ai-agents).

### 7.2 Problem: “Another agent is not just a tool”
Tool invocation protocols don’t capture peer collaboration: negotiation, progress, long-running workflows, partial results, and multi-turn coordination.  
**A2A mechanism:** the Task model, task lifecycle states, streaming/subscription, push notifications, and artifact outputs formalize agent collaboration patterns rather than single-shot function calls [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/), [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).

### 7.3 Problem: Long-running work + humans-in-the-loop
Enterprise processes often require approvals, user input, delegated authentication, or multi-step workflows that don’t fit synchronous APIs.  
**A2A mechanism:** interrupted states like input_required and auth_required, plus the ability to stream progress and/or notify asynchronously, are built into the state machine and operations [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/), [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/).

### 7.4 Problem: Cross-system UX/format mismatch (multi-modality)
Different agents and hosts may support different modalities (text-only, structured JSON, file transfer, embedded UI).  
**A2A mechanism:** messages are composed of Parts with media types and multiple data representations; agents can tailor outputs to accepted modes [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/). Google explicitly positions “parts” as enabling UX negotiation [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/).

### 7.5 Problem: Secure discovery and secure delegation at scale
When agents discover other agents at runtime, spoofed endpoints and tampered capability documents become risks.  
**A2A mechanism:** signed Agent Cards (integrity), extended authenticated cards (least exposure), declared security schemes, and enterprise alignment in the spec’s guiding principles [[4]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/releases), [[94]](https://a2a-protocol.org/v0.3.0/specification/), [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/). Google explicitly emphasizes secure-by-default design and enterprise auth parity [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/).

### 7.6 Problem: Transport heterogeneity in real enterprises
Enterprises may require gRPC, REST, or JSON-RPC depending on stack, compliance, and tooling.  
**A2A mechanism:** formal bindings plus a canonical data model intended to preserve semantics across bindings [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/). Conformance tooling explicitly tests multi-transport equivalence [[20]](https://github.com/a2aproject/a2a-tck).

### 7.7 Problem: Ecosystem evolution without fragmentation
Standards often fail when vendors add incompatible features.  
**A2A mechanism:** URI-identified, negotiable extensions that can be required or optional; plus explicit constraints that extensions cannot mutate core structures/enums [[11]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/topics/extensions/).

---

## 8) Where MCP and A2A *overlap* (and why they’re still different)

### 8.1 Both use JSON-RPC patterns and capability negotiation—but for different layers
- MCP: JSON-RPC is the protocol substrate; negotiation happens during `initialize` and controls which primitives/features can be used (tools/resources/prompts/sampling/etc.) [[30]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/lifecycle), [[26]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25).  
- A2A: JSON-RPC is one binding among others; negotiation is largely via discovery metadata (Agent Card) and extensions activation headers [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[11]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/topics/extensions/), with task/agent semantics on top.

### 8.2 Both are converging on “durable async work,” but from different starting points
- A2A started task-first (long-running by design) [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/), [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/).  
- MCP introduced experimental tasks later to support durable requests with polling and deferred retrieval [[42]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/changelog), [[101]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/utilities/tasks).  

They are conceptually convergent here, but operationally different: A2A tasks are the core unit of agent collaboration; MCP tasks are an optional wrapper to make tool/server operations durable.

### 8.3 Both face similar security classes—but at different boundaries
- MCP’s big risk surface is tool poisoning/prompt injection via tool metadata and outputs; MCP itself warns that tool descriptions/annotations are untrusted unless from trusted servers and emphasizes explicit user consent controls [[26]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25), [[32]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/tools).  
- A2A’s analogous risk is cross-agent prompt injection via Agent Cards/messages/artifacts; the A2A samples repo explicitly warns to treat all external agent inputs (including AgentCard fields) as untrusted and sanitize before using in prompts [[75]](https://github.com/a2aproject/a2a-samples).

---

## 9) Open issues / debates (practical realities as of Feb 2026)

### 9.1 A2A: discovery beyond well-known endpoints (registry still evolving)
A2A clearly supports well-known Agent Card discovery, and there are active discussions/proposals for an **agent registry** with entitlements, search, and access controls—reflecting enterprise needs for inventory and policy gating across many agents [[79]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/discussions/741). This is still an evolving design area.

### 9.2 A2A: streaming “delta” semantics and client expectations
Community issues indicate that streaming chunk semantics (how to represent partial message deltas vs finalized messages in task history) can be tricky; one request proposed an explicit delta indicator because storing each chunk as a full message can prematurely close streams or bloat history [[12]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/issues/822). The protocol’s evolution (e.g., removing `final` in v1.0 RC task status update events) also changes how stream termination is detected [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/), [[95]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/raw/refs/tags/v0.3.0/specification/grpc/a2a.proto).

### 9.3 A2A: enterprise operational hardening is partly “outside the protocol”
A2A positions itself as enterprise-ready and includes security/observability intentions in principles [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), but third-party enterprise platform commentary argues production deployments still need strong infrastructure for mTLS, retries, rate limiting, observability, and policy enforcement around A2A endpoints [[76]](https://www.diagrid.io/blog/making-agent-to-agent-a2a-communication-secure-and-reliable-with-dapr). In practice, many organizations will layer a service mesh/API gateway/policy engine around A2A.

### 9.4 MCP: transport complexity debates (SSE + sessions)
MCP’s Streamable HTTP is highly specified (reconnect, resumability, session IDs, Origin validation) [[37]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/transports), but it has drawn criticism from implementers as complex compared to WebSockets or simpler stateless HTTP patterns (community issues) [[38]](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/493), [[82]](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/236). Maintainers have not adopted some proposed simplifications (e.g., “not planned” resolution) [[82]](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/modelcontextprotocol/issues/236).

### 9.5 MCP: security incidents and tool ecosystem risks
Independent security research and vendor guidance highlight risks such as command injection in MCP servers, tool poisoning, confused deputy attacks, and session/token issues (the protocol emphasizes consent but cannot enforce UI/security at the protocol layer) [[26]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25), [[58]](https://www.elastic.co/security-labs/mcp-tools-attack-defense-recommendations), [[59]](https://github.com/microsoft/mcp-for-beginners/blob/main/02-Security/README.md). This matters for A2A+MCP hybrid systems: if an A2A agent internally relies on MCP servers, MCP server hygiene becomes part of the A2A agent’s supply chain risk.

---

## 10) Practical guidance: when to use A2A, MCP, or both

### Choose **A2A** when you need:
- multi-agent collaboration across teams/vendors/frameworks,
- runtime discovery and task delegation to remote agents,
- long-running workflows with status, interruption states, artifacts, and push notifications [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/), [[93]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/definitions/),
- multi-binding support (JSON-RPC/gRPC/REST) and conformance expectations (TCK) [[1]](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/), [[20]](https://github.com/a2aproject/a2a-tck).

### Choose **MCP** when you need:
- a standard way for an LLM app/agent to integrate **tools/resources/prompts** with typed schemas [[32]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/tools), [[33]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/resources), [[34]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/server/prompts),
- host-mediated permissions, isolation, and consistent tool calling across many servers [[29]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/architecture),
- standardized transports (stdio for local tools; Streamable HTTP for remote) and standardized OAuth-based authorization for HTTP deployments [[37]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/transports), [[44]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/authorization),
- ecosystem discoverability via a registry with namespace controls [[110]](https://blog.modelcontextprotocol.io/posts/2025-09-08-mcp-registry-preview/), [[109]](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/modelcontextprotocol/registry/refs/heads/main/docs/reference/server-json/official-registry-requirements.md).

### Choose **both (common in enterprises)** when you need:
- *Agents that collaborate via A2A* while *each agent uses MCP* to access its internal tools/data, and/or
- a bridge where A2A orchestration is the uniform outer layer and MCP servers are wrapped behind A2A agents for compatibility and governance [[71]](https://a2aprotocol.ai/docs/guide/a2a-mcp-integration), [[70]](https://github.com/sing1ee/a2a-mcp-openrouter).

In short: **MCP is your “tool/context plug.” A2A is your “agent collaboration bus.”** They are different layers—and are increasingly used together rather than as substitutes [[3]](https://developers.googleblog.com/en/a2a-a-new-era-of-agent-interoperability/), [[63]](https://auth0.com/blog/mcp-vs-a2a/), [[2]](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A).
